Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - - DEBERTA: DECODING ENHANCED BERT WITH DIS ENTANGLED ATTENTION
**Pengcheng He**[1] **, Xiaodong Liu**[2] **, Jianfeng Gao**[2] **, Weizhu Chen**[1] 1 Microsoft Dynamics 365 AI 2 Microsoft Research

{penhe,xiaodl,jfgao,wzchen}@microsoft.com

## ABSTRACT

Recent progress in pre-trained neural language models has significantly improved the performance of many natural language processing (NLP) tasks. In this paper we propose a new model architecture **DeBERTa** ( **D** ecoding- **e** nhanced **BERT** with disentangled **a** ttention) that improves the BERT and RoBERTa models using two novel techniques. The first is the disentangled attention mechanism, where each word is represented using two vectors that encode its content and position, respectively, and the attention weights among words are computed using disentangled matrices on their contents and relative positions, respectively. Second, an enhanced mask decoder is used to incorporate absolute positions in the decoding layer to predict the masked tokens in model pre-training. In addition, a new virtual adversarial training method is used for fine-tuning to improve models’ generalization. We show that these techniques significantly improve the efficiency of model pre-training and the performance of both natural language understand (NLU) and natural langauge generation (NLG) downstream tasks. Compared to RoBERTa-Large, a DeBERTa model trained on half of the training data performs consistently better on a wide range of NLP tasks, achieving improvements on MNLI by +0.9% (90.2% vs. 91.1%), on SQuAD v2.0 by +2.3% (88.4% vs. 90.7%) and RACE by +3.6% (83.2% vs. 86.8%). Notably, we scale up DeBERTa by training a larger version that consists of 48 Transform layers with 1.5 billion parameters. The significant performance boost makes the single DeBERTa model surpass the human performance on the SuperGLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2019a) for the first time in terms of macro-average score (89.9 versus 89.8), and the ensemble DeBERTa model sits atop the SuperGLUE leaderboard as of January 6, 2021, outperforming the human baseline by a decent margin (90.3 versus 89.8). The pre-trained DeBERTa models and the source code were released at: https://github.com/microsoft/DeBERTa[1] .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The Transformer has become the most effective neural network architecture for neural language modeling. Unlike recurrent neural networks (RNNs) that process text in sequence, Transformers apply self-attention to compute in parallel every word from the input text an attention weight that gauges the influence each word has on another, thus allowing for much more parallelization than RNNs for large-scale model training (Vaswani et al., 2017). Since 2018, we have seen the rise of a set of large-scale Transformer-based Pre-trained Language Models (PLMs), such as GPT (Radford et al., 2019; Brown et al., 2020), BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), RoBERTa (Liu et al., 2019c), XLNet (Yang et al., 2019), UniLM (Dong et al., 2019), ELECTRA (Clark et al., 2020), T5 (Raffel et al., 2020), ALUM (Liu et al., 2020), StructBERT (Wang et al., 2019c) and ERINE (Sun et al., 2019) . These PLMs have been fine-tuned using task-specific labels and created new state of the art in many downstream natural language processing (NLP) tasks (Liu et al., 2019b; Minaee et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020; He et al., 2019a;b; Shen et al., 2020).
> 1Our code and models are also available at HuggingFace Transformers: https://github.com/ huggingface/transformers, https://huggingface.co/models?filter=deberta

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

In this paper, we propose a new Transformer-based neural language model **DeBERTa** ( **D** ecoding- **e** nhanced **BERT** with disentangled **a** ttention), which improves previous state-of-the-art PLMs using two novel techniques: a disentangled attention mechanism, and an enhanced mask decoder.

**Disentangled attention.** Unlike BERT where each word in the input layer is represented using a vector which is the sum of its word (content) embedding and position embedding, each word in DeBERTa is represented using two vectors that encode its content and position, respectively, and the attention weights among words are computed using disentangled matrices based on their contents and relative positions, respectively. This is motivated by the observation that the attention weight of a word pair depends on not only their contents but their relative positions. For example, the dependency between the words “deep” and “learning” is much stronger when they occur next to each other than when they occur in different sentences.

**Enhanced mask decoder.** Like BERT, DeBERTa is pre-trained using masked language modeling (MLM). MLM is a fill-in-the-blank task, where a model is taught to use the words surrounding a mask token to predict what the masked word should be. DeBERTa uses the content and position information of the context words for MLM. The disentangled attention mechanism already considers the contents and relative positions of the context words, but not the absolute positions of these words, which in many cases are crucial for the prediction. Consider the sentence “a new store opened beside the new mall” with the italicized words “store” and “mall” masked for prediction. Although the local contexts of the two words are similar, they play different syntactic roles in the sentence. (Here, the subject of the sentence is “store” not “mall,” for example.) These syntactical nuances depend, to a large degree, upon the words’ absolute positions in the sentence, and so it is important to account for a word’s absolute position in the language modeling process. DeBERTa incorporates absolute word position embeddings right before the softmax layer where the model decodes the masked words based on the aggregated contextual embeddings of word contents and positions.
In addition, we propose a new virtual adversarial training method for fine-tuning PLMs to downstream NLP tasks. The method is effective in improving models’ generalization.

We show through a comprehensive empirical study that these techniques substantially improve the efficiency of pre-training and the performance of downstream tasks. In the NLU tasks, compared to RoBERTa-Large, a DeBERTa model trained on half the training data performs consistently better on a wide range of NLP tasks, achieving improvements on MNLI by +0.9% (90.2% vs. 91.1%), on SQuAD v2.0 by +2.3%(88.4% vs. 90.7%), and RACE by +3.6% (83.2% vs. 86.8%). In the NLG tasks, DeBERTa reduces the perplexity from 21.6 to 19.5 on the Wikitext-103 dataset. We further scale up DeBERTa by pre-training a larger model that consists of 48 Transformer layers with 1.5 billion parameters. The single 1.5B-parameter DeBERTa model substantially outperforms T5 with 11 billion parameters on the SuperGLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2019a) by 0.6%(89.3% vs. 89.9%), and surpasses the human baseline (89.9 vs. 89.8) for the first time. The ensemble DeBERTa model sits atop the SuperGLUE leaderboard as of January 6, 2021, outperforming the human baseline by a decent margin (90.3 versus 89.8).

## 2 BACKGROUND

## 2.1 TRANSFORMER

A Transformer-based language model is composed of stacked Transformer blocks (Vaswani et al., 2017). Each block contains a multi-head self-attention layer followed by a fully connected positional feed-forward network. The standard self-attention mechanism lacks a natural way to encode word position information. Thus, existing approaches add a positional bias to each input word embedding so that each input word is represented by a vector whose value depends on its content and position. The positional bias can be implemented using absolute position embedding (Vaswani et al., 2017; Radford et al., 2019; Devlin et al., 2019) or relative position embedding (Huang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019). It has been shown that relative position representations are more effective for natural language understanding and generation tasks (Dai et al., 2019; Shaw et al., 2018). The proposed disentangled attention mechanism differs from all existing approaches in that we represent each input word using two separate vectors that encode a word’s content and position, respectively, and

## 3.2 ENHANCED MASK DECODER ACCOUNTS FOR ABSOLUTE WORD POSITIONS

DeBERTa is pretrained using MLM, where a model is trained to use the words surrounding a mask token to predict what the masked word should be. DeBERTa uses the content and position information of the context words for MLM. The disentangled attention mechanism already considers the contents and relative positions of the context words, but not the absolute positions of these words, which in many cases are crucial for the prediction.

Given a sentence “a new **store** opened beside the new **mall** ” with the words “store” and “mall” masked for prediction. Using only the local context (e.g., relative positions and surrounding words) is insufficient for the model to distinguish _store_ and _mall_ in this sentence, since both follow the word _new_ with the same relative positions. To address this limitation, the model needs to take into account absolute positions, as complement information to the relative positions. For example, the subject of the sentence is “store” not “mall”. These syntactical nuances depend, to a large degree, upon the words’ absolute positions in the sentence.

There are two methods of incorporating absolute positions. The BERT model incorporates absolute positions in the input layer. In DeBERTa, we incorporate them right after all the Transformer layers but before the _softmax_ layer for masked token prediction, as shown in Figure 2. In this way, DeBERTa captures the relative positions in all the Transformer layers and only uses absolute positions as complementary information when decoding the masked words. Thus, we call DeBERTa’s decoding component an Enhanced Mask Decoder (EMD). In the empirical study, we compare these two methods of incorporating absolute positions and observe that EMD works much better. We conjecture that the early incorporation of absolute positions used by BERT might undesirably hamper the model from learning sufficient information of relative positions. In addition, EMD also enables us to introduce other useful information, in addition to positions, for pre-training. We leave it to future work.

## 4 SCALE INVARIANT FINE-TUNING

This section presents a new virtual adversarial training algorithm, Scale-invariant-Fine-Tuning (SiFT), a variant to the algorithm described in Miyato et al. (2018); Jiang et al. (2020), for fine-tuning.

Virtual adversarial training is a regularization method for improving models’ generalization. It does so by improving a model’s robustness to adversarial examples, which are created by making small perturbations to the input. The model is regularized so that when given a task-specific example, the model produces the same output distribution as it produces on an adversarial perturbation of that example.

For NLP tasks, the perturbation is applied to the word embedding instead of the original word sequence. However, the value ranges (norms) of the embedding vectors vary among different words and models. The variance gets larger for bigger models with billions of parameters, leading to some instability of adversarial training.

5

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Inspired by layer normalization (Ba et al., 2016), we propose the SiFT algorithm that improves the training stability by applying the perturbations to the _normalized_ word embeddings. Specifically, when fine-tuning DeBERTa to a downstream NLP task in our experiments, SiFT first normalizes the word embedding vectors into stochastic vectors, and then applies the perturbation to the normalized embedding vectors. We find that the normalization substantially improves the performance of the fine-tuned models. The improvement is more prominent for larger DeBERTa models. Note that we **only** apply SiFT to DeBERTa1 _._ 5 _B_ on SuperGLUE tasks in our experiments and we will provide a more comprehensive study of SiFT in our future work.

## 5 EXPERIMENT

This section reports DeBERTa results on various NLU tasks.

## 5.1 MAIN RESULTS ON NLU TASKS

Following previous studies of PLMs, we report results using large and base models.

## 5.1.1 PERFORMANCE ON LARGE MODELS

Table 1: Comparison results on the GLUE development set.

We pre-train our large models following the setting of BERT (Devlin et al., 2019), except that we use the BPE vocabulary of Radford et al. (2019); Liu et al. (2019c). For training data, we use Wikipedia (English Wikipedia dump[3] ; 12GB), BookCorpus (Zhu et al., 2015) (6GB), OPENWEBTEXT (public Reddit content (Gokaslan & Cohen, 2019); 38GB), and STORIES (a subset of CommonCrawl (Trinh & Le, 2018); 31GB). The total data size after data deduplication (Shoeybi et al., 2019) is about 78G. Refer to Appendix A.2 for a detailed description of the pre-training dataset.
We use 6 DGX-2 machines (96 V100 GPUs) to train the models. A single model trained with 2K batch size and 1M steps takes about 20 days. Refer to Appendix A for the detailed hyperparamters.

We summarize the results on eight NLU tasks of GLUE (Wang et al., 2019b) in Table 1, where DeBERTa is compared DeBERTa with previous Transform-based PLMs of similar structures (i.e. 24 layers with hidden size of 1024) including BERT, RoBERTa, XLNet, ALBERT and ELECTRA. Note that RoBERTa, XLNet and ELECTRA are pre-trained on 160G training data while DeBERTa is pretrained on 78G training data. RoBERTa and XLNet are pre-trained for 500K steps with 8K samples in a step, which amounts to four billion training samples. DeBERTa is pre-trained for one million steps with 2K samples in each step. This amounts to two billion training samples, approximately half of either RoBERTa or XLNet. Table 1 shows that compared to BERT and RoBERTa, DeBERTa performs consistently better across all the tasks. Meanwhile, DeBERTa outperforms XLNet in six out of eight tasks. Particularly, the improvements on MRPC (1.1% over XLNet and 1.0% over RoBERTa), RTE (2.4% over XLNet and 1.7% over RoBERTa) and CoLA (1.5% over XLNet and 2.5% over RoBERTa) are significant. DeBERTa also outperforms other SOTA PLMs, i.e., ELECTRAlarge and XLNetlarge, in terms of average GLUE score.

Among all GLUE tasks, MNLI is most often used as an indicative task to monitor the research progress of PLMs. DeBERTa significantly outperforms all existing PLMs of similar size on MNLI and creates a new state of the art.

For comparison, we include ALBERTxxlarge (Lan et al., 2019)[4] and Megatron (Shoeybi et al., 2019) with three different model sizes, denoted as Megatron336M, Megatron1.3B and Megatron3.9B, respectively, which are trained using the same dataset as RoBERTa. Note that Megatron336M has a similar model size as other models mentioned above[5] .

We summarize the results in Table 2. Compared to the previous SOTA PLMs with a similar model size (i.e., BERT, RoBERTa, XLNet, ALBERTlarge, and Megatron336M), DeBERTa shows superior performance in all seven tasks. ## 5.2 MODEL ANALYSIS

In this section, we first present an ablation study to quantify the relative contributions of different components introduced in DeBERTa. Then, we study the convergence property to characterize the model training efficiency. We run experiments for analysis using the base model setting: a model is pre-trained using the Wikipedia + Bookcorpus dataset for 1M steps with batch size 256 in 7 days on a DGX-2 machine with 16 V-100 GPUs. Due to space limit, we visualize the different attention patterns of DeBERTa and RoBERTa in Appendix A.7.

## 5.2.1 ABLATION STUDY

To verify our experimental setting, we pre-train the RoBERTa base model from scratch. The re-pretrained RoBERTa model is denoted as RoBERTa-ReImpbase. To investigate the relative contributions of different components in DeBERTa, we develop three variations:

- -EMD is the DeBERTa base model without EMD.

- -C2P is the DeBERTa base model without the content-to-position term ((c) in Eq. 4).
- -P2C is the DeBERTa base model without the position-to-content term ((b) in Eq. 4). As XLNet also uses the relative position bias, this model is close to XLNet plus EMD.

Table 4: Ablation study of the DeBERTa base model.

Table 4 summarizes the results on four benchmark datasets. First, RoBERTa-ReImp performs similarly to RoBERTa across all benchmark datasets, verfiying that our setting is reasonable. Second, we see that removing any one component in DeBERTa results in a sheer performance drop. For instance, removing EMD (-EMD) results in a loss of 1.4% (71.7% vs. 70.3%) on RACE, 0.3% (92.1% vs. 91.8%) on SQuAD v1.1, 1.2% (82.5% vs. 81.3%) on SQuAD v2.0, 0.2% (86.3% vs. 86.1%) and 0.1% (86.2% vs. 86.1%) on MNLI-m/mm, respectively. Similarly, removing either _content-to-position_ or _position-to-content_ leads to inferior performance in all the benchmarks. As expected, removing two components results in even more substantial loss in performance.

## 5.3 SCALE UP TO 1.5 BILLION PARAMETERS

Larger pre-trained models have shown better generalization results (Raffel et al., 2020; Brown et al., 2020; Shoeybi et al., 2019). Thus, we have built a larger version of DeBERTa with 1.5 billion parameters, denoted as DeBERTa1 _._ 5 _B_ . The model consists of 48 layers with a hidden size of 1,536 and 24 attention heads[6] . DeBERTa1 _._ 5 _B_ is trained on a pre-training dataset amounting to 160G, similar to that in Liu et al. (2019c), with a new vocabulary of size 128K constructed using the dataset.

To train DeBERTa1 _._ 5 _B_ , we optimize the model architecture as follows. First, we share the projection matrices of relative position embedding _Wk,r, Wq,r_ with _Wk,c, Wq,c_ , respectively, in all attention layers to reduce the number of model parameters. Our ablation study in Table 13 on base models shows that the projection matrix sharing reduces the model size while retaining the model performance.

Second, a convolution layer is added aside the first Transformer layer to induce n-gram knowledge of sub-word encodings and their outputs are summed up before feeding to the next Transformer layer[7] .

Table 5 reports the test results of SuperGLUE (Wang et al., 2019a) which is one of the most popular NLU benchmarks. SuperGLUE consists of a wide of NLU tasks, including Question Answering (Clark et al., 2019; Khashabi et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018), Natural Language Inference (Dagan et al., 2006; Bar-Haim et al., 2006; Giampiccolo et al., 2007; Bentivogli et al., 2009), Word Sense Disambiguation (Pilehvar & Camacho-Collados, 2019), and Reasoning (Levesque et al., 2011; Roemmele et al., 2011). Since its release in 2019, top research teams around the world have been developing large-scale PLMs that have driven striking performance improvement on SuperGLUE.

The significant performance boost due to scaling DeBERTa to a larger model makes the single DeBERTa1 _._ 5 _B_ surpass the human performance on SuperGLUE for the first time in terms of macroaverage score (89.9 versus 89.8) as of December 29, 2020, and the ensemble DeBERTa model (DeBERTa _Ensemble_ ) sits atop the SuperGLUE benchmark rankings as of January 6, 2021, outperforming the human baseline by a decent margin (90.3 versus 89.8). Compared to T5, which consists of 11 billion parameters, the 1.5-billion-parameter DeBERTa is much more energy efficient to train and maintain, and it is easier to compress and deploy to apps of various settings.

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

This paper presents a new model architecture DeBERTa (Decoding-enhanced BERT with disentangled attention) that improves the BERT and RoBERTa models using two novel techniques. The first is the disentangled attention mechanism, where each word is represented using two vectors that encode its content and position, respectively, and the attention weights among words are computed using disentangled matrices on their contents and relative positions, respectively. The second is an enhanced mask decoder which incorporates absolute positions in the decoding layer to predict the masked tokens in model pre-training. In addition, a new virtual adversarial training method is used for fine-tuning to improve model’s generalization on downstream tasks.

We show through a comprehensive empirical study that these techniques significantly improve the efficiency of model pre-training and the performance of downstream tasks. The DeBERTa model with 1.5 billion parameters surpasses the human performance on the SuperGLUE benchmark for the first time in terms of macro-average score.

DeBERTa surpassing human performance on SuperGLUE marks an important milestone toward general AI. Despite its promising results on SuperGLUE, the model is by no means reaching the human-level intelligence of NLU. Humans are extremely good at leveraging the knowledge learned from different tasks to solve a new task with no or little task-specific demonstration. This is referred to as _compositional generalization_ , the ability to generalize to novel compositions (new tasks) of familiar constituents (subtasks or basic problem-solving skills). Moving forward, it is worth exploring how to make DeBERTa incorporate compositional structures in a more explicit manner, which could allow combining neural and symbolic computation of natural language similar to what humans do.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents DeBERTa, a significant evolution of the BERT/RoBERTa architectures that addresses key limitations in how Transformer models handle positional information. The quality of the work is exceptionally high, evidenced by its rigorous empirical validation and the landmark achievement of surpassing human parity on the SuperGLUE benchmark. The clarity of the writing is a major strength; the authors provide intuitive, linguistically grounded motivations for their architectural choices—specifically, identifying that while relative positions are crucial for semantic dependencies (disentangled attention), absolute positions are vital for syntactic role identification (enhanced mask decoder). The most significant issue, common to many high-impact PLM papers, is the immense computational requirement for training the 1.5B parameter variant, which may limit reproducibility for researchers without industrial-scale resources. Additionally, while the paper introduces a virtual adversarial training method for fine-tuning, the high-level introduction focuses primarily on the architectural changes, leaving the specific synergy between the architecture and the adversarial training slightly underspecified in the opening sections. Overall, this is a top-tier contribution that effectively combines architectural innovation with state-of-the-art performance.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The submitted paper provides a compelling and well-motivated advancement to transformer-based architectures, effectively grounding the proposed "DeBERTa" model within the existing literature while clearly articulating its contributions. The clarity of the technical exposition—particularly the conceptual distinction between content and position embeddings—is a significant strength, and the impressive empirical results on the SuperGLUE benchmark provide strong evidence for the model's architectural superiority. However, from a rigorous reviewer's perspective, the paper would benefit from a more nuanced discussion of the computational overhead introduced by the disentangled attention mechanism. While the authors demonstrate efficiency in terms of training data requirements, the mathematical complexity of calculating these disentangled attention matrices compared to standard attention could impact latency; exploring the tradeoff between performance gains and inference-time complexity would bolster the paper’s practical significance. Additionally, while the empirical results are robust, the paper relies heavily on performance metrics to drive its narrative; supplementing this with qualitative analysis or ablation studies that isolate the specific contributions of the "enhanced mask decoder" versus the "disentangled attention" would provide a more granular understanding of why this architecture achieves such disparate performance improvements across tasks.

comments.title: Vague Specification of Projection Matrix Sharing
comments.quote: Section 59
comments.explanation: The claim regarding the sharing of projection matrices $W_{k,r}, W_{q,r}$ with $W_{k,c}, W_{q,c}$ is technically underspecified. The paper fails to explain how input features from different vector spaces (hidden state dimension vs. position embedding dimension) are reconciled to allow for matrix sharing.

comments.title: Inconsistent Range Definition for Relative Distance
comments.quote: Paragraph 20
comments.explanation: The text defines relative distance $\delta(i, j)$ in the range $[0, 2k]$. This range is mathematically inconsistent with the requirement to differentiate between preceding and succeeding tokens (which typically requires a range such as $[-k, k]$), casting doubt on the implementation of the relative position logic.

comments.title: Hyperbolic Claims Regarding Performance and Efficiency
comments.quote: Paragraph 9 (SuperGLUE performance), Paragraph 41 (Training samples efficiency)
comments.explanation: The paper characterizes a 0.6% performance gain as 'substantial' and frames reduced training samples as a direct efficiency gain without accounting for increased computational costs (FLOPs) per token in the disentangled architecture, potentially misleading readers on the model's comparative utility.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The submitted paper provides a compelling and well-motivated advancement to transformer-based architectures, effectively grounding the proposed "DeBERTa" model within the existing literature while clearly articulating its contributions. The clarity of the technical exposition—particularly the conceptual distinction between content and position embeddings—is a significant strength, and the impressive empirical results on the SuperGLUE benchmark provide strong evidence for the model's architectural superiority. However, from a rigorous reviewer's perspective, the paper would benefit from a more nuanced discussion of the computational overhead introduced by the disentangled attention mechanism. While the authors demonstrate efficiency in terms of training data requirements, the mathematical complexity of calculating these disentangled attention matrices compared to standard attention could impact latency; exploring the tradeoff between performance gains and inference-time complexity would bolster the paper’s practical significance. Additionally, while the empirical results are robust, the paper relies heavily on performance metrics to drive its narrative; supplementing this with qualitative analysis or ablation studies that isolate the specific contributions of the "enhanced mask decoder" versus the "disentangled attention" would provide a more granular understanding of why this architecture achieves such disparate performance improvements across tasks.

comments.title: Mathematical Notation Error in Masked Language Modeling Objective
comments.quote: Specifically, given a sequence _**X**_ “ t _xi_ u, we corrupt it into _X_ ˜ by masking 15% of its tokens at random and then train a language model parameterized by _θ_ to reconstruct _**X**_ by predicting the masked tokens _x_ ˜ conditioned on _**X**_ [˜] :
comments.explanation: The OCR rendering here is highly garbled (e.g., '“ t _xi_ u', '_X_ ˜', 'X [˜]'). While I recognize the intent is to define the standard MLM cross-entropy loss objective (predicting x_i given the corrupted context), the notation is currently unintelligible for a formal definition in an academic paper. Even accounting for OCR, the formula is missing, failing to provide the mathematical definition the surrounding text introduces.

comments.title: Discrepancy in Scaling Factor Notation
comments.quote: Finally, we apply a scaling factor of ~~?~~ 13 _d_[on] _**A**_ **[˜]** .
comments.explanation: The text references a scaling factor necessary for stability, which in standard Transformer literature (Vaswani et al., 2017) is 1/sqrt(d_k). The OCR output '~~?~~ 13 _d_' is clearly a mapping error for a fraction involving the square root of the dimension. While the intent is clear from the reference to Vaswani et al., the lack of a coherent mathematical expression makes the implementation detail vague.

comments.title: Ambiguous/Incomplete Algorithm 1 Formatting
comments.explanation: The algorithmic block is severely damaged by OCR errors, to the point where multiple lines have been merged into single, incoherent sequences (e.g., '4:3: **for** _i_ _**A**_ **˜**...'). As this is the core methodological contribution of the paper (the efficient implementation of Disentangled Attention), the inability to parse the loop indices or the matrix multiplications makes this section non-reproducible in its current provided state.

comments.title: Inconsistent Equation Referencing in Section 3
comments.quote: The position-to-content term is calculated as _**Kj[c][Q][r] δ**_ p _**j,i**_ q⊺.
comments.explanation: The notation used in the prose to describe the calculation of the position-to-content term contains mismatched indices and OCR-induced formatting issues ('_**Kj[c][Q][r]**_'). While the prose correctly explains the logic (querying relative position embedding with the key content), the formal expression is syntactically broken, making it difficult to verify the matrix dimensions or transpose operations.

comments.title: OCR-corrupted Scaling Factor Notation
comments.quote: Finally, we apply a scaling factor of ?? 13 d[on] A [˜] .

While the intent is clear from the reference (Vaswani et al., 2017) and common knowledge in the field, the presentation is broken and effectively unreadable for a student or researcher trying to verify the exact normalization applied. This falls under notation errors caused by the OCR process that impair the formal definition.

comments.title: Ambiguous/Garbled Algorithm 1 notation
comments.quote: 19: AH ˜ “ o “ A softmax c Ñ c ` A p ?? cA ˜ 3Ñ d p[q] ` [V][c] Ap Ñ c
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 is highly garbled due to OCR, making the final computation step (line 19) incoherent. Specifically, the summation and the application of the softmax to the disentangled attention scores are presented with symbols like '??' and '3Ñ', obscuring the matrix operation. While the logic is conceptually clear (the sum of disentangled matrices passed through softmax), the algorithmic definition itself is not reconstructible from this text alone, which is a significant barrier for reproducibility.

comments.title: Inconsistency in index definition for Relative Distance
comments.quote: Denote k as the maximum relative distance, δ p i, j q P r0 , 2 k q as the relative distance from token i to token j
comments.explanation: The text states that the relative distance δ(i, j) is in the range [0, 2k]. However, relative distance is typically defined in an interval like [-k, k] to differentiate between preceding and succeeding tokens. If the range were purely [0, 2k], the model would lose the directional distinction between 'i before j' and 'j before i', which contradicts the subsequent discussion of position-to-content and content-to-position needing to account for directionality (e.g., distinguishing δ(i, j) vs δ(j, i)). This likely reflects an OCR misread or a poorly defined index shift.

comments.title: Algorithmic notation and index inconsistency in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: 11:12: for j A ˜ c Ñ 0 c , ..., N r: , j s ´ K 1 do c r j, :s Q ⊺ r 13: end for 14: for j “ 0 , ..., N ´ 1 do 15: for i “ 0 , ..., N ´ 1 do 16: Ap Ñ c r i, j s “ A ˜ p Ñ c r δ r j, i s , j s
comments.explanation: The OCR of the 'Efficient Implementation' pseudocode in Algorithm 1 contains significant breakage that exceeds simple formatting errors. Specifically, the loops and matrix indexing logic for the position-to-content term (lines 11–18) are garbled to the point where the relationship between indices $i$ and $j$ and the relative position matrix $\delta$ is ambiguous. While the prose in Section 3.1 correctly describes that $Q_r$ and $K_c$ are used for this step, the provided Algorithm 1 code as listed is logically unintelligible. This hinders the reproducibility of the efficient implementation, a core technical contribution of the paper.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding SuperGLUE performance
comments.quote: The single 1.5B-parameter DeBERTa model substantially outperforms T5 with 11 billion parameters on the SuperGLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2019a) by 0.6%(89.3% vs. 89.9%)
comments.explanation: The authors compare the 1.5B DeBERTa model (89.9) to T5-11B (89.3). While they claim DeBERTa 'substantially outperforms' T5, the gap is 0.6 percentage points. In the context of large-scale language model benchmarks, describing a 0.6% improvement as 'substantial'—especially when comparing a model that is an order of magnitude smaller—is hyperbolic and potentially misleading regarding the relative efficiency versus performance trade-off.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of matrix dimensions in text vs equations
comments.quote: $P \in R[2][k][ˆ][d]$ represents the relative position embedding vectors
comments.explanation: The text refers to $P \in R[2][k][ˆ][d]$ while defining the maximum relative distance as $k$. In Transformer relative position implementations (e.g., Shaw et al.), the matrix size is typically $(2k+1) \times d$ or $2k \times d$ to encompass the full range of distances $[-k, k]$. While context implies $2k$ is the span, the notation lacks the inclusion of the 'zero' distance index, which is standard in these implementations. Combined with the OCR error in the notation, this leaves the specific indexing of the relative distance matrix $\delta(i, j)$ ambiguous.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in position-to-content term description
comments.quote: The position-to-content term is calculated as $K_{j,c}Q_{r, \delta(j,i)}^\top$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the text states 'The position-to-content term is calculated as $K_{j,c}Q_{r, \delta(j,i)}^\top$', but the previous paragraph and Algorithm 1 define this operation as using the query $Q_r$ and key $K_c$. Specifically, the attention score $\tilde{A}_{i,j}$ includes the term $Q_{\delta(j,i),r}K_{j,c}^\top$. This textual description swaps the vectors $K_c$ and $Q_r$ compared to the formula in equation (4). While this might be a typo in the prose reflecting a misunderstanding of the indices, it contradicts the mathematical definition established earlier.

comments.title: Numerical/Data misinterpretation regarding training efficiency
comments.quote: RoBERTa and XLNet are pre-trained for 500K steps with 8K samples in a step, which amounts to four billion training samples. DeBERTa is pre-trained for one million steps with 2K samples in each step. This amounts to two billion training samples, approximately half of either RoBERTa or XLNet.
comments.explanation: The prose claims the DeBERTa training amount is 'half' of the other models. While the total number of samples is indeed half (2B vs 4B), the claim of efficiency needs to be contextualized. A reader might assume this implies a 2x efficiency gain per sample; however, DeBERTa's disentangled attention and EMD have higher computational costs per token than standard self-attention. The paper does not explicitly compare the total wall-clock time training compute (FLOPs) required for these 2B vs 4B samples, which could mislead the reader on the relative training efficiency.

comments.title: Unclear variable definition in ablation study
comments.quote: -P2C is the DeBERTa base model without the position-to-content term ((b) in Eq. 4).
comments.explanation: The text references '(b) in Eq. 4', but Eq. 4 in the provided context is a single multi-term summation line for $\tilde{A}_{i,j}$. The equation does not label components as (a), (b), or (c). While a reader can infer that (b) refers to the $Q_{r}K_{c}^\top$ component based on the phrasing, the lack of explicit labeling in the referenced equation is a source of potential ambiguity.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'summed up' in convolution layer modification
comments.quote: Second, a convolution layer is added aside the first Transformer layer to induce n-gram knowledge of sub-word encodings and their outputs are summed up before feeding to the next Transformer layer
comments.explanation: The text states the outputs of the convolution layer and the first Transformer layer are 'summed up'. However, in standard Transformer architectures, the first layer output typically has an embedding dimension $d$. If the convolution layer (designed to capture n-gram knowledge) outputs a feature map of a potentially different shape, dimension-matching is required for a summation operation. Given that $d=1536$ for the 1.5B model, it is unclear if the convolution preserves this dimensionality or if a projection is implied. While this is likely an element-wise addition, 'summed up' is technically underspecified regarding the alignment and transformation of these disparate sources of information.

comments.title: Potential numerical discrepancy in score comparison
comments.quote: The single 1.5B-parameter DeBERTa model substantially outperforms T5 with 11 billion parameters on the SuperGLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2019a) by 0.6%(89.3% vs. 89.9%)
comments.explanation: In the introduction, the text claims the improvement is 0.6% (89.9 vs 89.3). However, looking at the SuperGLUE leaderboard (Table 5), the T5 11B model score is listed as 89.3. Subtracting 89.3 from 89.9 yields 0.6. While the arithmetic is correct, the claim that 0.6% constitutes 'substantially' outperforming a model 7x larger is an overstatement of the magnitude of the improvement given the context of SOTA benchmarks where 0.6 points are often within variance or represent marginal gains.

comments.title: Implicit assumption of parameter sharing efficiency
comments.quote: First, we share the projection matrices of relative position embedding $Wk,r, Wq,r$ with $Wk,c, Wq,c$, respectively, in all attention layers to reduce the number of model parameters.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that sharing these projection matrices reduces model parameters while 'retaining' model performance, referencing an ablation study (Table 13). However, $W_{k,c}$ and $W_{k,r}$ (similarly for queries) project input hidden states and relative position embeddings respectively. These inputs typically reside in different vector spaces (hidden dimension $d$ vs position embedding dimension). If they share a matrix, it implies a forced compatibility between these spaces or a re-interpretation of the position embeddings' dimensionality. The explanation is too vague for a researcher to reproduce the implementation without further detail on the specific dimensionality constraints imposed by this 'sharing'.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in the disentangled attention score equation
comments.quote: $\tilde{A}_{i,j} = Q_{i,c}K_{j,c}^\top + Q_{i,c}K_{\delta(i,j),r}^\top + Q_{\delta(j,i),r}K_{j,c}^\top$
comments.explanation: Looking at the provided summary and the text in Section 3, the formula for the disentangled self-attention score is defined with three terms. Throughout the text, the authors describe the attention as a 'sum of four attention scores' (content-to-content, content-to-position, position-to-content, and position-to-position). The authors explicitly state: 'Since we use relative position embedding, the position-to-position term does not provide much additional information and is removed from equation 2 in our implementation.' This clarifies why there are only three terms in the displayed equation. However, the initial claim that the mechanism decomposes into 'four components' followed by an explicit subtraction of one component from the formal model creates a slight ambiguity for the reader regarding whether the formula is technically 'decomposed' or specifically 'truncated'. While context resolves the discrepancy, the label 'four components' in the prose combined with the 3-term equation is a slight logical mismatch.

comments.title: Ambiguity in relative distance index notation
comments.quote: $Q_{\delta(j,i),r}K_{j,c}^\top$
comments.explanation: The text explains the position-to-content term as $K_{j,c}Q_{\delta(j,i),r}^\top$ in the notation paragraph ('The position-to-content term is calculated as $K_{j,c}Q_{\delta(j,i),r}^\top$'), yet the main equation uses the transposed form $Q_{\delta(j,i),r}K_{j,c}^\top$. Given the matrix dimensions $Q \in R^{N \times d}$ and $K \in R^{N \times d}$, both are mathematically valid depending on the implementation of the projection, but the switch in order between the prose and the equation can confuse the reader regarding which represents the query and which represents the key. The context implies that the authors are consistent in their goal (computing the dot product), but the variable ordering inconsistency is technically problematic.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents DeBERTa, a highly motivated architecture that effectively decouples content and position representations to address known limitations in BERT-style models, with the disentangled attention mechanism and enhanced mask decoder offering logical and promising improvements to pre-training dynamics. The core technical intuition is clearly articulated, particularly the argument that relative positions suffice for representation learning while absolute positions remain critical for decoding, a distinction well-supported by the provided examples. However, the paper requires immediate attention to presentation quality; there are distracting typographical errors in the title and abstract (e.g., "Dis entangled," "natural langauge," and "natural language understand"), and the introduction is somewhat repetitive between the abstract and the motivation section. Additionally, while virtual adversarial training is listed as a key contribution, its novelty and mechanism are not sufficiently outlined in the introduction to gauge its impact relative to the architectural innovations. Finally, the provided text truncates abruptly in Section 2.1, preventing verification of the background setup and mathematical formalization, though the content reviewed suggests a technically sound direction with significant benchmark improvements.

comments.title: Typographical errors in acronym expansions
comments.quote: natural language understand (NLU) and natural langauge generation (NLG)
comments.explanation: The text reads 'natural language understand (NLU) and natural langauge generation (NLG)'. 'Understand' should be 'understanding' to correctly form the standard expansion of the widely recognized acronym NLU. Similarly, 'langauge' is a clear typographical error for 'language'. I checked the context and standard NLP terminology; these are straightforward OCR/drafting slips that do not alter the technical claims or methodology.

comments.title: Incorrect noun form for architecture name
comments.quote: consists of 48 Transform layers with 1.5 billion parameters
comments.explanation: The authors use 'Transform layers' as a compound noun phrase. In transformer-based literature, the correct term is 'Transformer layers' (referring to the architectural module introduced by Vaswani et al., 2017). This is a minor grammatical/typographical issue that does not affect the model specification or numerical results.

comments.title: Likely OCR/typographical errors in cited model names and references
comments.quote: ALUM (Liu et al., 2020), StructBERT (Wang et al., 2019c) and ERINE (Sun et al., 2019)
comments.explanation: The acronym 'ALUM' does not correspond to a known pre-trained language model and is highly likely an OCR or typing error for 'ALBERT' (Lan et al., 2019). Furthermore, the citation '(Liu et al., 2020)' appears mismatched for ALBERT. Similarly, 'ERINE' is almost certainly a typo for 'ERNIE' (Sun et al., 2019). While these appear in a non-essential enumeration of related work, they could confuse a careful reader or lead to incorrect literature retrieval. I verified against standard PLM naming conventions and concluded these are transcription artifacts rather than intentional designations.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for relative position representation
comments.quote: For a token at position _i_ in a sequence, we represent it using two vectors, t _**Hi**_ u and t _**Pi**_ | _**j**_ u, which represent its content and relative position with the token at position _j_ , respectively.
comments.explanation: The notation P_{i|j} suggests that the position vector for token i is conditionally dependent on the target token j, which would imply an O(N^2 d) storage requirement for position embeddings. This appears inconsistent with the later definition in Section 3.1 where P ∈ R^{2k × d} is introduced as a shared relative position embedding matrix. However, reviewing Section 3.1.1 and Algorithm 1 clarifies the authors' intent: they use a single shared lookup table indexed by relative distance δ(i,j) during attention computation, rather than storing pairwise position vectors. The surrounding context resolves the mathematical inconsistency, but the initial notation is misleading and could confuse readers about the memory complexity and architectural design.

comments.title: Misleading terminology in SiFT procedure description
comments.quote: Specifically, when fine-tuning DeBERTa to a downstream NLP task in our experiments, SiFT first normalizes the word embedding vectors into stochastic vectors, and then applies the perturbation to the normalized embedding vectors.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'stochastic vectors' is almost certainly a typo for 'normalized vectors' or 'unit-norm vectors'. Normalization is a deterministic mathematical operation that scales vectors to a fixed magnitude or variance; it does not introduce randomness. Since SiFT is a proposed training procedure, this terminology error could mislead readers about the algorithm's mechanics. The intended meaning is clearly that embeddings are normalized deterministically before adding stochastic (random) perturbations, as is standard in Virtual Adversarial Training. Correcting this term is necessary to accurately describe the method and ensure reproducible implementation.

comments.title: Typo/Ambiguity in Section 4: 'stochastic vectors'
comments.quote: SiFT first normalizes the word embedding vectors into stochastic vectors, and then applies the perturbation to the normalized embedding vectors.
comments.explanation: Initially, the phrase 'into stochastic vectors' stood out as contradictory to the immediate context. The preceding sentence explicitly grounds SiFT in 'layer normalization' and states the method 'normalizes the word embedding vectors.' I checked the surrounding text and the standard definition of layer normalization, confirming it is a deterministic scaling operation (typically mapping to zero mean and unit variance). The use of 'stochastic' implies the introduction of probability or randomness, which clashes with both the method's stated goal (stability via deterministic normalization) and the fact that the adversarial perturbation is applied *after* this step. Even assuming the most plausible intended word ('normalized' or 'unit'), the current wording introduces unnecessary confusion about whether randomness is part of the normalization itself. This appears to be a drafting or OCR typo, but it remains a minor ambiguity that a careful reader might briefly question before resolving from context.

comments.title: Factual discrepancy regarding prior work's training steps
comments.quote: RoBERTa and XLNet are pre-trained for 500K steps with 8K samples in a step, which amounts to four billion training samples.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that RoBERTa was pre-trained for 500K steps. However, the original RoBERTa paper (Liu et al., 2019) explicitly states that the large model was trained for 1 million steps with a batch size of 8K (amounting to 8 billion tokens). I initially considered whether this referred to a specific ablation or a corrected implementation, but the primary citation describes the 1M-step setup. This discrepancy affects the baseline for the subsequent claim that DeBERTa's two billion 'training samples' are 'approximately half' of the prior work. While the internal arithmetic (500K × 8K = 4B) is mathematically sound, the external factual claim contradicts the cited literature, which could mislead readers comparing data efficiency or compute budgets.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'training samples' terminology
comments.quote: which amounts to four billion training samples. DeBERTa is pre-trained for one million steps with 2K samples in each step.
comments.explanation: The authors use 'training samples' to describe the product of training steps and batch size. In the context of Masked Language Modeling, this undoubtedly refers to individual tokens, but the term is never explicitly defined. 'Samples' can sometimes imply sentences, passages, or document-level sequences, which would make the stated numbers off by orders of magnitude. I checked the surrounding text and found that 'tokens' is used elsewhere (e.g., 'masked tokens' in the Abstract), confirming the authors' intent. The arithmetic only holds if 'samples' = 'tokens'. While a domain expert would immediately resolve this, a less specialized reader could reasonably misinterpret the scale of the pre-training corpus. Explicitly stating 'training tokens' would improve precision.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in Abstract regarding SQuAD v2.0 results
comments.explanation: The abstract claims that DeBERTa-Large improves over RoBERTa-Large on SQuAD v2.0 by +2.3%, citing scores of 88.4% vs. 90.7%. However, Table 2 explicitly reports RoBERTa_large's SQuAD v2.0 F1 score as 89.4%, and DeBERTa_large's as 90.7%. The actual improvement is 1.3%, not 2.3%, and the baseline score in the abstract (88.4%) does not match the table (89.4%). This appears to be a typographical error, likely confusing the F1 score with the exact match score (86.5%) or simply mistyping the digit. While it does not alter the methodological claims or overall conclusion that DeBERTa outperforms RoBERTa, it introduces a factual discrepancy between the summary and the reported results that should be corrected for accuracy.

comments.explanation: The abstract claims that DeBERTa-Large improves over RoBERTa-Large on SQuAD v2.0 by +2.3%, citing scores of 88.4% vs. 90.7%. However, Table 2 explicitly reports RoBERTa_large's SQuAD v2.0 F1 score as 89.4%, and DeBERTa_large's as 90.7%. The actual improvement is 1.3%, not 2.3%, and the baseline score in the abstract (88.4%) does not match the table (89.4%). This appears to be a typographical error, likely confusing the F1 score with the exact match score (86.5%) or simply mistyping the digit. While it does not alter the methodological claims or overall conclusion that DeBERTa outperforms RoBERTa, it introduces a factual discrepancy between the summary and the reported results that should be corrected for accuracy.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents DeBERTa, a technically sound architecture that advances the state-of-the-art in pre-trained language models through improved efficiency and performance. The authors articulate a clear motivation for two key innovations—a disentangled attention mechanism that decouples content and relative position information to better capture dependencies, and an enhanced mask decoder that incorporates absolute positions into the decoding layer—which are explained with sufficient clarity in the introduction. The experimental results demonstrate that these methods yield substantial gains, outperforming RoBERTa-Large on multiple benchmarks with half the training data and achieving top-tier results on SuperGLUE. A significant issue that will require rigorous justification is the computational overhead and architectural complexity of the proposed mechanism compared to standard baselines, as the reported efficiency improvements must be carefully validated to ensure they are not merely an artifact of the proposed virtual adversarial training strategy.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in relative position directionality
comments.quote: Note that we use δ_{j,i} rather than δ_{i,j} here. This is because for a given position i, position-to-content computes the attention weight of the key content at j with respect to the query position at i, thus the relative distance is δ_{j,i}.
comments.explanation: The authors define δ_{i,j} as the relative distance 'from token i to token j' with a range {-k, ..., 2k}, which implies a signed metric where δ_{i,j} likely corresponds to the vector difference j-i. Later, for the position-to-content term (Query i, Key j), they claim to use δ_{j,i} instead of δ_{i,j}. If δ_{i,j} = j-i, then δ_{j,i} = i-j = -δ_{i,j}. Using the opposite sign/direction creates a logical contradiction with the stated definition of 'distance with respect to the query position at i,' unless there is a non-standard definition of δ used in the omitted equation (Eq. 4). This could confuse a reader attempting to reconstruct the mathematical model.

comments.title: Incorrect parameter comparison: Megatron1.3B size
comments.quote: Although Megatron1.3B is three times larger than DeBERTa, DeBERTa outperforms it in three of the four benchmarks.
comments.explanation: The text attempts to compare DeBERTa Large (1.5 billion parameters) with Megatron1.3B (1.3 billion parameters) and claims the latter is 'three times larger.' Mathematically, 1.3B is not 3 * 1.5B (4.5B). Even if comparing Megatron336M (0.336B) to DeBERTa_base (~0.11B), the factor is incorrect. The statement is internally inconsistent with the parameter counts provided in the text and Table 2.

comments.title: Incorrect claim on number of tasks outperformed
comments.quote: Meanwhile, DeBERTa outperforms XLNet in six out of eight tasks.
comments.explanation: The text claims a win rate of 6/8 against XLNet in Table 1. However, examining the results in Table 1: DeBERTa_large wins on CoLA, SST-2, STS-B, QNLI, and RTE (5 tasks). It loses on MNLI, QQP, and MRPC (3 tasks). The text's claim of 'six out of eight' is factually incorrect based on the data presented in the same passage.

comments.title: Underspecified Convolutional Layer Architecture
comments.quote: Second, a convolution layer is added aside the first Transformer layer to induce n-gram knowledge of sub-word encodings and their outputs are summed up before feeding to the next Transformer layer[7] .
comments.explanation: The authors describe a key architectural modification to the base model (adding a convolution layer to induce n-gram knowledge) in a vague manner. The phrase 'added aside' is ambiguous—is it inserted in parallel, in series, or within the residual stream? More critically, the description lacks technical specifications required for reproduction, such as kernel size, stride, padding, activation function, and exact dimensional matching with the Transformer outputs. These details are essential to understand the mechanism of 'inducing n-gram knowledge' and ensuring compatibility with the 'summed up' output. While an appendix reference [7] likely contains these details, the lack of an explicit definition in the main text makes the method underspecified.

comments.explanation: The first instance of 'sca​​le up' in the first paragraph contains a stray diacritical mark (or OCR artifact), appearing as 'sca​​le up'. This is likely a formatting artifact rather than a meaningful error. It does not impede understanding of the text, but it is inconsistent with standard writing conventions.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
### High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound contribution to the field of pre-trained language models, introducing two core innovations—disentangled attention and an enhanced mask decoder—that are clearly explained and empirically validated. The writing is generally clear and logically structured, effectively grounding the contributions in prior work (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) and providing compelling quantitative results, including the notable achievement of surpassing human baselines on SuperGLUE. However, a significant issue is the lack of a rigorous or formal description of the disentangled attention mechanism itself within the provided excerpt; the conceptual motivation is strong, but one would expect to see the precise mathematical formulation (e.g., how the disentangled content-to-content, content-to-position, position-to-content, and position-to-position attention scores are computed and combined) early in the paper. Additionally, while the paper claims superior efficiency (e.g., training on half the data of RoBERTa-Large), the provided excerpt does not offer a detailed comparison of computational cost, training time, or model throughput, which would be essential for substantiating this claim. Finally, from a clarity standpoint, the abrupt introduction of virtual adversarial training in the abstract and introduction without further elaboration in the excerpt leaves the reader wondering about its role and integration into the overall framework. For a paper of this caliber aiming for a top venue like ICLR, ensuring that all key technical novelties are formally defined and that efficiency claims are rigorously supported with quantitative evidence is critical.

comments.title: Misleading comparison of training data half
comments.quote: a DeBERTa model trained on half of the training data performs consistently better on a wide range of NLP tasks
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'half of the training data' but the later context (Section 5.1.1) describes a 78GB dataset. Compared to RoBERTa, which was trained on 160GB of data (from Liu et al., 2019c), 'half' is approximately correct (78 vs 160). However, the phrasing 'half of the training data' could be interpreted as half of some specific dataset, which is ambiguous. The context partially resolves this by describing the actual dataset size, but the abstract alone is misleading.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about outperforming T5
comments.quote: The single 1.5B-parameter DeBERTa model substantially outperforms T5 with 11 billion parameters on the SuperGLUE benchmark (Wang et al., 2019a) by 0.6%(89.3% vs. 89.9%)
comments.explanation: The text says outperforms 'by 0.6%' but the numbers in parentheses show 89.3% vs 89.9%, which is a 0.6 percentage point improvement, not a 0.6% relative improvement. The percentage point difference is correct, but the phrasing 'by 0.6%' could be interpreted as relative improvement. In context, this is a minor imprecision common in ML literature.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of normalization in SiFT: 'stochastic vectors'
comments.quote: SiFT first normalizes the word embedding vectors into stochastic vectors, and then applies the perturbation to the normalized embedding vectors.
comments.explanation: The term 'stochastic vectors' is non-standard in this context and could mislead. The authors state the method is inspired by layer normalization, which produces normalized vectors (zero mean, unit variance) — these are deterministic given the input, not stochastic. The later phrase 'normalized embedding vectors' clarifies that normalization is applied, but the qualifier 'stochastic' suggests randomness (e.g., from dropout or noise), which is not explained. A careful reader might wonder if the normalization includes a random component or if 'stochastic' is a typo for 'standardized' or 'normalized'. This ambiguity could affect reproducibility of the fine-tuning method.

comments.title: Redundant phrase: 'DeBERTa is compared DeBERTa'
comments.quote: We summarize the results on eight NLU tasks of GLUE (Wang et al., 2019b) in Table 1, where DeBERTa is compared DeBERTa with previous Transform-based PLMs of similar structures (i.e. 24 layers with hidden size of 1024) including BERT, RoBERTa, XLNet, ALBERT and ELECTRA.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'DeBERTa is compared DeBERTa' appears to contain a duplication. The intended reading is likely 'DeBERTa is compared with previous Transform-based PLMs...'. The repeated 'DeBERTa' is an error (possibly a copy-paste or OCR artifact) that disrupts the sentence flow and could confuse a reader. The surrounding context makes the intended meaning clear, but this is a real prose error that should be corrected.

comments.title: Table 1 citation: ALBERT listed in prose but not shown in provided table
comments.quote: including BERT, RoBERTa, XLNet, ALBERT and ELECTRA
comments.explanation: The prose mentions ALBERT as being compared in Table 1, but the provided excerpt of Table 1 only includes rows for BERT_large, RoBERTa_large, XLNet_large, ELECTRAlarge, and DeBERTa_large. ALBERT_large is not shown. It is possible that ALBERT was omitted from the excerpt or that the table is incomplete in the OCR extraction. Since the full paper context was not provided for the entire table, this could simply be a missing piece of the table. However, a careful reader would note the discrepancy between the prose claim and the visible table rows. This is a minor inconsistency that could be resolved if the full table were available.
